configuration/outlook-configuration.html.title: Outlook Address Book Configuration | User Docs
configuration/outlook-configuration.html.summary: Outlook Address Book Configuration This guide explains how to configure the Outlook address book provider to fetch contacts from Microsoft Outlook. Overview The Outlook provider allows you to retrieve contact information from Outlook address books and use it within Word documents for mail merge, data insertion, or other purposes. Prerequisites Microsoft Outlook must be installed on the system Outlook must be configured with at least one profile The add-in must have permission to access Outlook (check Outlook Trust Center settings) Configuration Basic Configuration Add the following configuration to your dataset configuration file: { "$schema": "../../../output/schemas/DatasetProviderConfiguration.schema.json", "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "OutlookContacts": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 30, "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Name", "EmailAddress": "Email", "CompanyName": "Company", "JobTitle": "Title", "BusinessPhone": "Phone", "MobilePhone": "Mobile" }, Required Properties $connectionType Value: "Outlook" Description: Specifies that this dataset uses the Outlook provider OutlookAddressbook Type: string Required: Yes Description: The name of the Outlook address book to access Common Values: "Contacts" - Your personal contacts folder "Global Address List" - Organization's GAL (Exchange environments) Custom address book names configured in Outlook ConnectionSettings Type: string Description: Reserved for future use (currently unused but required) Default: "" SQLQuery Type: string Description: Reserved for future filtering capabilities Default: "" Optional Properties Timeout Type: integer Default: 30 Description: Maximum time in seconds to wait for Outlook operations FieldAliasOrder Type: Dictionary<string, string> Description: Maps internal field names to friendly display names Available Fields: FullName - Complete name from address book FirstName - First name (if available) LastName - Last name (if available) EmailAddress - Email address CompanyName - Company/organization name JobTitle - Position/title BusinessPhone - Business telephone number MobilePhone - Mobile phone number BusinessAddress - Business address Type - Entry type (Contact, Exchange User, etc.) UseLocalCache Type: boolean Default: false Description: Enable caching to improve performance for large address books LocalCachePath Type: string Description: Path for cached data (supports environment variables) Example: "%temp%\\outlook_cache" Examples Example 1: Personal Contacts { "PersonalContacts": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 30, "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Contact Name", "EmailAddress": "Email", "CompanyName": "Company", "BusinessPhone": "Phone" } } } Example 2: Global Address List (Exchange) { "CompanyDirectory": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 60, "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Employee Name", "EmailAddress": "Email Address", "JobTitle": "Position", "CompanyName": "Department" }, "UseLocalCache": true, "LocalCachePath": "%localappdata%\\KutanaBrochet\\Cache" } } Example 3: Multiple Address Books { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "Clients": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Client Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "" }, "Staff": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Company Staff", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "" }, "External": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "" } } } } Finding Your Address Book Names To find the exact names of your Outlook address books: Open Microsoft Outlook Click on Home tab > Address Book button (or press Ctrl+Shift+B) In the Address Book window, use the dropdown at the top The dropdown shows all available address book names - use these exact names in your configuration Common address book names: Contacts - Your personal Outlook contacts Global Address List - Company directory (Exchange/Microsoft 365) Suggested Contacts - Auto-collected contacts Any custom address books you've created Returned Data Structure The Outlook provider returns a DataSet with the following columns: Column Name Data Type Description FullName string Complete display name FirstName string First name (when available) LastName string Last name (when available) EmailAddress string Primary email address CompanyName string Company/organization name JobTitle string Job title/position BusinessPhone string Business telephone number MobilePhone string Mobile phone number BusinessAddress string Business address Type string Entry type identifier Note: Not all fields may be populated for every contact, depending on what information is available in Outlook. Troubleshooting "Microsoft Outlook is not installed" Ensure Microsoft Outlook is installed on the system Verify that Outlook is properly licensed "Address book 'X' not found" Check the exact spelling of the address book name (case-sensitive) Open Outlook's Address Book (Ctrl+Shift+B) to verify available names Ensure you have permission to access the address book "Access denied to Outlook" Open Outlook Go to File > Options > Trust Center > Trust Center Settings Select Programmatic Access Ensure appropriate settings allow programmatic access You may need to approve access when prompted "No entries found in address book" Verify the address book contains contacts Check if you have read permissions for the address book For Exchange GAL, ensure you're connected to the server Performance Issues with Large Address Books Enable UseLocalCache in configuration Increase the Timeout value Consider filtering contacts (future feature via SQLQuery) Security Considerations The add-in requires programmatic access to Outlook Outlook may prompt users to grant access when first connecting Cached data is stored locally - ensure appropriate file permissions Consider data privacy when using Global Address Lists Cached data is not encrypted - use secure storage locations Best Practices Use Descriptive Dataset Names: Name datasets clearly (e.g., "ClientContacts", "StaffDirectory") Enable Caching for Large Lists: Use local cache for GAL or large contact lists Map Fields Appropriately: Use FieldAliasOrder to provide user-friendly column names Set Appropriate Timeouts: Increase timeout for large address books or slow networks Test with Small Address Books First: Verify configuration with personal contacts before using GAL Future Enhancements The following features are planned for future releases: SQLQuery Support: Filter contacts based on criteria Incremental Updates: Only fetch changed contacts Custom Field Mapping: Support for custom Outlook contact fields Multiple Profile Support: Specify which Outlook profile to use

configuration/outlook-implementation-sum.title: Outlook Address Book Integration - Implementation Summary | User Docs
configuration/outlook-implementation-sum.summary: Outlook Address Book Integration - Implementation Summary Overview I've successfully implemented the Outlook address book connection for your Office Add-in. The implementation allows you to fetch contacts from Microsoft Outlook address books and use them within Word documents for mail merge, data insertion, or other purposes. Changes Made 1. Updated OutlookProvider.cs Location: src\Core\Infrastructure.Persistence\ExternalData\Providers\Outlook.cs Key Implementation Details: COM Interop: Uses late binding (dynamic) to interact with Outlook's COM objects Multi-Framework Support: Conditional compilation for .NET Framework 4.8 and .NET 9 .NET 4.8: Uses Marshal.GetActiveObject to connect to running Outlook instance .NET 9: Uses Type.GetTypeFromProgID and Activator.CreateInstance Address Book Retrieval: Connects to Outlook via MAPI namespace Searches for the specified address book by name Retrieves all entries from the address book Data Extraction: Handles both Outlook Contacts and Exchange Users Extracts 10 fields: FullName, FirstName, LastName, EmailAddress, CompanyName, JobTitle, BusinessPhone, MobilePhone, BusinessAddress, Type Gracefully handles missing data (returns empty strings) COM Object Management: Properly releases all COM objects to prevent memory leaks Uses try-finally blocks to ensure cleanup Forces garbage collection after releasing COM objects Error Handling: Detailed error messages for common issues Handles: Outlook not installed, address book not found, access denied, COM exceptions Configuration Required Configuration Properties The OutlookDto class requires the following properties: { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", // Required: Name of the address book "ConnectionSettings": "", // Reserved for future use "SQLQuery": "", // Reserved for future filtering "Timeout": 30, // Optional: Timeout in seconds "FieldAliasOrder": { // Optional: Map column names "FullName": "Contact Name", "EmailAddress": "Email" }, "UseLocalCache": false, // Optional: Enable caching "LocalCachePath": "" // Optional: Cache file location } Configuration Examples 1. Basic Personal Contacts Configuration { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "MyContacts": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 30 } } } } 2. Global Address List (Exchange) { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "CompanyDirectory": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "Timeout": 60, "UseLocalCache": true, "LocalCachePath": "%temp%\\outlook_gal_cache" } } } } 3. Multiple Address Books with Field Mapping { "DatasetProviderConfiguration": { "Datasets": { "Clients": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Client Contacts", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Client Name", "EmailAddress": "Email", "CompanyName": "Organization", "BusinessPhone": "Phone" } }, "Staff": { "$connectionType": "Outlook", "OutlookAddressbook": "Global Address List", "ConnectionSettings": "", "SQLQuery": "", "FieldAliasOrder": { "FullName": "Employee", "EmailAddress": "Company Email", "JobTitle": "Position" } } } } } Data Structure The Outlook provider returns a DataSet with the following columns: Column Name Data Type Description FullName string Complete display name from address book FirstName string First name (when available from contact details) LastName string Last name (when available from contact details) EmailAddress string Primary email address CompanyName string Company/organization name JobTitle string Job title/position BusinessPhone string Business telephone number MobilePhone string Mobile phone number BusinessAddress string Business address Type string Entry type identifier Note: Not all fields may be populated for every contact, depending on what information is available in Outlook. How to Find Your Address Book Names Open Microsoft Outlook Click Home tab > Address Book button (or press Ctrl+Shift+B) In the Address Book window, use the dropdown at the top The dropdown shows all available address book names - use these exact names in your configuration Common address book names: Contacts - Your personal Outlook contacts Global Address List - Company directory (Exchange/Microsoft 365) Suggested Contacts - Auto-collected contacts Custom address books you've created Security Considerations Outlook Trust Center Users may need to configure Outlook to allow programmatic access: Open Outlook Go to File > Options > Trust Center > Trust Center Settings Select Programmatic Access Ensure appropriate settings allow programmatic access Users may need to approve access when first connecting (Outlook will prompt) Data Privacy Cached data is stored locally unencrypted - ensure secure storage locations Be mindful when using Global Address Lists - respect company data policies The add-in requires programmatic access to Outlook Troubleshooting "Microsoft Outlook is not installed" Solution: Install Microsoft Outlook on the system "Address book 'X' not found" Solution: Check exact spelling (case-sensitive), use Address Book dialog in Outlook to verify name "Access denied to Outlook" Solution: Configure Trust Center settings, approve access when prompted "No entries found in address book" Solution: Verify the address book contains contacts and you have read permissions Performance Issues with Large Address Books Solution: Enable UseLocalCache in configuration Increase the Timeout value (e.g., to 60 or 120 seconds) For GAL, consider using during off-peak hours Best Practices Descriptive Dataset Names: Use clear names like "ClientContacts", "StaffDirectory" Enable Caching for Large Lists: Use local cache for GAL or large contact lists to improve performance Field Mapping: Use FieldAliasOrder to provide user-friendly column names Appropriate Timeouts: Set timeout based on address book size: Small (< 100 contacts): 30 seconds (default) Medium (100-1000 contacts): 60 seconds Large (> 1000 contacts or GAL): 120+ seconds Test with Small Address Books First: Verify configuration with personal contacts before using GAL Future Enhancements The following features are reserved for future implementation: SQLQuery Support: Filter contacts based on criteria (currently reserved but not implemented) ConnectionSettings: Additional connection parameters (currently reserved) Incremental Updates: Only fetch changed contacts Custom Field Mapping: Support for custom Outlook contact fields Multiple Profile Support: Specify which Outlook profile to use Documentation I've created comprehensive documentation: Configuration Guide: docs/user/configuration/outlook-configuration.md Complete user documentation with examples Troubleshooting guide Security considerations Step-by-step instructions Example Configuration: docs/user/configuration/outlook-dataset-example.json Three complete examples: Personal contacts Global Address List Custom address book Testing Recommendations Unit Tests: Create tests for OutlookProvider.Create() validation Integration Tests: Test against a test Outlook profile with known contacts Performance Tests: Test with varying address book sizes to set appropriate timeouts Error Handling Tests: Test error scenarios: Outlook not running Invalid address book name Access denied Empty address book Usage Example After configuration, users can access Outlook contacts from your Word Add-in: // In your application code var query = new GetDataset.Query("MyContacts"); var result = queryDispatcher.Dispatch<GetDataset.Query, DataSet>(query); if (result.IsSuccess) { var contacts = result.Value.Tables[0]; // Use the contacts data... } Summary The Outlook address book integration is now complete and ready for use. The implementation: ✅ Connects to Microsoft Outlook via COM Interop ✅ Supports both .NET Framework 4.8 and .NET 9 ✅ Retrieves contacts from any Outlook address book ✅ Properly manages COM objects to prevent memory leaks ✅ Provides comprehensive error handling ✅ Supports field mapping and caching for performance ✅ Includes complete user documentation Users can now configure the add-in to pull contact data directly from their Outlook address books for use in Word documents.

configuration/ribbon.html.title: Ribbon | User Docs
configuration/ribbon.html.summary: Ribbon The file Ribbon.xml is used for the configuration of all the buttons on the Ribbon. Where this config file is located There can be a ribbon.xml file for each Office applications. There needs to be aseparate Ribbon.xml file for each application which is located in the following locations: %appdata%\Kutana+Brochet\<OfficeApp Name>\config eg ...\AppData\Roaming\Kutana+Brochet\Word\config or %localappdata%\Kutana+Brochet\<OfficeApp Name>\config The file located in the %localappdata% (..\Local\..) will take precedent on the one in %appdata% (..\Roaming\..). In this situation the file in Local will be used and the file in Roaming will be ignored. (see Using Configuration layering for the configuration files for more details on laying) XML Schema This file is written in XML and conforms the standard Microsoft Office Ribbon XML schema. The integration with Kutana+Brochet is through actions using the following five element parts: id image onAction getVisible getEnabled The Kutana+Brochet Coms addin contains a set of built in images. The list of images available can be seen in the following GitHub link: https://github.com/kutana-brochet/office-addins/tree/master/src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Resources Example style Button Below is the XML for a button on the ribbon for applying the style Level 1 Heading to the selected text: <button id="OperativeLevel1Heading" image="Level 1 Heading" onAction="RunScript" label="Level 1 Heading" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" screentip="Level 1 Heading - " supertip="Applies the first level heading style eg 1. in bold and ALLCAPS." /> Notes on each item: id="OperativeLevel1Heading" - this is only relevant when the onAction is "RunScript". This is used by the configuration setting "ScriptConfiguration" "Mappings"to define the commands to be run and when the button if visible and enabled. NB: The id needs to be unique for each ribbon element. To have two buttons that run the same script there will need to be a Mapping for each id but these can run the same Script. image="Level 1 Heading" - this is used to define the image to be used and displayed on the ribbon. The image can either be an inbuilt resource in the COMS addin or one generated by the system when Word is loaded. See image below https://github.com/kutana-brochet/office-addins/tree/master/src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Resources onAction="RunScript" - defines the command to be run. See onAction below for the for list of available commands getVisible - determines when the ribbon element is visible. Available callbacks: Callback Used With Matches Against GetVisibleByScript Script buttons (onAction="RunScript") ScriptConfiguration.Mappings by button id GetVisibleById Any ribbon element RulesConfiguration.RibbonItemRules by element id GetVisibleByTag Any ribbon element RulesConfiguration.RibbonItemRules by element tag See Script Configuration for script-based visibility. See Ribbon Item Rules for rules-based visibility. getEnabled - determines when the ribbon element is clickable. Available callbacks: Callback Used With Matches Against GetEnabledByScript Script buttons (onAction="RunScript") ScriptConfiguration.Mappings by button id GetEnabledById Any ribbon element RulesConfiguration.RibbonItemRules by element id GetEnabledByTag Any ribbon element RulesConfiguration.RibbonItemRules by element tag See Script Configuration for script-based enabled logic. See Ribbon Item Rules for rules-based enabled logic. image In the ribbon XML defined in a Word template any ribbon icon used in the image line needs to be added as a Resource to the Word template file. When using the Kutana+Brochet COMs addin these ribbon icons can either be added as a Resource in the COMSs addin for generated on the fly when Word is loaded. The embedded image incons The embedded list of icons can be found using the following link: https://github.com/kutana-brochet/office-addins/tree/master/src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Resources or they can be seen on the following Dropdown area: The list of icons embebbed on version ??? are: C:\Brochet Dropbox\Brochet internal\Development\Graphics\20251202 - MSI V7 Ribbon - Blob.png - BodyText1.png - BodyText1Blue.png - BodyText2.png [To be completed] Generating icons when Word is load Icon images can also be generated when Word is loaded.

features/RibbonIcons.html.title: RibbonIcons | User Docs
features/RibbonIcons.html.summary: RibbonIcons "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json" Description The JSON element "RibbonIcons" is used for defining the icons that are auto-generated when Word loads. The is one elements of "RibbonIcons": "Icons" Recommended JSON file name It is recommended that the JSON file holding this information is called StyleButtonsConfig.json. This section of the configuration can be divided between two separate files eg StyleButtonsConfigA.json and StyleButtonsConfigB.json and then will both be read and the information joined together. Remember if there is a "Icons" that is defined in both files the "RibbonIcons" in the filename that in later in the alapbetic list StyleButtonsConfigB.json will overwrite the other. "RibbonIcons" This section is used for defining the icon image. Example Below is an example of two Mappings for the Party 1 and Background 1 styles buttons: XML from the Ribbon.xml file: <box id="PartyBox1" boxStyle="vertical"> <button id="PartyParties" image="Parties 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Parties" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> <button id="PartyBackground" image="Background 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Background" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> </box> JSON from the configuration file StyleButtonsConfig.json Note: There are two settings for each icon the second being the same icon name except it has Dark appended to the name. This creates a black version of the same icons which is used when running Office in dark mode. Usually the ony difference between the two settings are the FontColour for example it is changed to White for dark mode. { "RibbonIcons": { "Icons": [ { "Name": "Parties 1", "Text": "(1)", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "Green", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 0, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, { "Name": "Parties 1Dark", "Text": "(1)", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "White", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 0, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, { "Name": "Background 1", "Text": "(A)", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "Green", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 2, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, { "Name": "Background 1Dark", "Text": "P", "Size": 32, "FontName": "Arial", "FontSize": 25, "FontStyles": [ "Regular" ], "FontColour": "White", "TextRenderingHint": "AntiAlias", "XOffset": 23, "YOffset": 0, "Contrast": 30 }, The following 11 elements are available for a ribbon icon: "Contrast" - not sure what this is but there are two setting 30 and 50. Think this is redundant!! "FontColour" - this is the list of colours that are available. The list of colour can be found in ???? How do we apply Hex colours "FontName" "FontSize" "FontStyle" "Name" "Size" "Text" "TextRenderingHint" "XOffset" "YOffset"

features/ScriptConfiguration.html.title: ScriptConfiguration | User Docs
features/ScriptConfiguration.html.summary: ScriptConfiguration "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json" Description The JSON element "ScriptConfiguration" is used for defining the "Scripts" that are run by ribbon buttons. These "Scripts" need to be linked back to the ribbon button by the "Mappings" section. The two elements of "ScriptConfiguration" are: "Mappings" "Scripts" Recommended JSON file name It is recommended that the JSON file holding this information is called Scripts.json. This section of the configuration can be divided between two separate files eg ScriptsStyleButtons.json and ScriptsMacroButtons.json and then will both be read and the information joined together. Remember if there is a "Mapping" that is defined in both files the "Mapping" in the filename that in later in the alapbetic list ScriptsStyleButtons.json will overwrite the other. "Mappings" This section is used for linking the ribbon elements (usually buttons) to the "Script" that will be run when the button is pressed. The secion also controls the button visibilty by linking to the and "RulesConfiguration" "Workflows" logic of when the ribbon element (usually button) is visible in the ribbon. Example Below is an example of two Mappings for the Party 1 and Background 1 styles buttons: XML from the Ribbon.xml file: <box id="PartyBox1" boxStyle="vertical"> <button id="PartyParties" image="Parties 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Parties" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> <button id="PartyBackground" image="Background 1" onAction="RunScript" label="Background" showLabel="false" getVisible="GetVisibleByScript" getEnabled="GetEnabledByScript" /> </box> JSON from the configuration file Scripts.json Note: the button id in the xml must match the name of the "Mappings" element name in the "Mappings" selction { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "ScriptConfiguration": { "Mappings": { "PartyParties": { "Script": "StyleParties", "EnabledWhen": "PartyGroupVisible", "VisibleWhen": "PartyGroupVisible" }, "PartyBackground": { "Script": "StyleBackground", "EnabledWhen": "PartyGroupVisible", "VisibleWhen": "PartyGroupVisible" }, There are three options of the "Mappings" element: "Script" - this is the name of the Script which is defined in the "Scripts": section (usually below the Mappings section of the "ScriptConfiguration" See the Scripts section below for a brief description. See the full documentation in Scripts.mp as there are a large number of options that can be used in the Scripts. "EnabledWhen" - this contains the name of the rules from "Rules" element of RulesConfiguration": { "Workflows": [{ "WorkflowName": "RibbonRules" See the EnabledWhen section below for a brief description. See the full documentation of RulesConfiguration.mp for documentation. "VisibleWhen" - this contains the name of the rules from "Rules" element of RulesConfiguration": { "Workflows": [{ "WorkflowName": "RibbonRules" See the EnabledWhen section below for a brief description. See the full documentation of RulesConfiguration.mp for documentation. "Scripts" The Scripts section is where the actions are defined when the ribbon button is pressed. See the full documentation in Scripts.md Example Scripts for the Party 1 and Background 1 buttons defined above: { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "ScriptConfiguration": { "Scripts": { "StyleParties": { "Comment": "Applies Parties Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Parties" } } ] }, "StyleBackground": { "Comment": "Applies Background Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Background" } } ] },

features/dms.html.summary: DMS The DMS.json file is for configuration of our Document Management System Integration. Schema Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Example Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. How to switch between different DMS options The K+B Word msi can integrate with different Document Management Systems (DMS). This is done using JSON configuration settings. For the DMS setting the JSON top level property "DocumentManagement": is used. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "DocumentManagement": { } } NB: "Provider" is the proptery for defining the DMS type Available DocumentManagement properties CreateOptions: this is used for debug testing PreProfileTemplates: (array) this holds the list of Templates to be pre-profiled (Front-end profiling). Each item can have the following properties: CloseOnCancel (Optional) DocType (Optional) Filter (Mandatory) Properties: ???What is this" PropertyMappings: contains the following 3 properties allowing information to be saved into the Word document: CustomProperties DocumentVariables DocumentProperties Provider: iManage, NetDocuments and None "PreProfileTemplates" properties This propery is used for listing the Word templates that are to be pre-profiled (Front-end profile). Filter: (Mandatory) this hold the name of the templates to be pre-profiled. It uses regular expressions. CloseOnCancel: true or false If set to true and the Cancel button is pressed on the DMS SaveAs screen then the initially created document is CLOSED. The default setting is false. DocType: used to pass to the DMS the document type to be set in the DMS SaveAs screen (currently only works in iManage - known bug in netDocs that information cannot be set in the SaveAs screen). **Example: ** A template called Letter is pre-profiled and if the cancel button is pressed then the initial document is closed. Any template that starts with "AB Company" is pre-profiled and if the cancel button is pressed then the document is not closed and the rest of the process (Filesite-New) is continued. "PreProfileTemplates": [ { "Filter": "Letter", "CloseOnCancel": true }, { "Filter": "AB Company*", "CloseOnCancel": false } ], "Properties" properties ??WHAT IS THIS USED FOR ??? "PropertyMappings" properties This property is used for defining the DMS information that is written into the Word document as either: Custom Document Properties, Variables or Document Properties. The three different properties are: CustomProperties, DocumentVariables and DocumentProperties. Each of these properties contain a list of paired values: "Name of variable": {{ DMSKeyName(s) and format }} "DocumentVariables": { "DMSAuthor": "{{ author.value }}", "DMSOurRef": "{{ profileInfo.identifier }}v{{ profileInfo.version | string.to_int | object.format \"d2\" }}" }, There can be multiple DMSKeyNames with additional joining text. The DMSKeyNames can be formatted using SCRIBAN formatting (https://github.com/scriban/scriban). Example: makes the document version into a 2 digit number but firstly converting the version string into an integer and then formatting this inro a 2 digit decimal number. "DMSOurRef": "{{ profileInfo.identifier }}v{{ profileInfo.version | string.to_int | object.format \"d2\" }}" Case sensitivity The top-level DMS key name (the first part before any .) is case-insensitive — {{ Matter }}, {{ matter }} and {{ MATTER }} all resolve to the same attribute. However, nested property names (after the .) are case-sensitive and must match exactly: Template Resolves? Why {{ author.value }} Yes Exact case match on property {{ AUTHOR.value }} Yes Top-level is case-insensitive {{ author.VALUE }} No Nested property name must match exact case {{ author.Value }} No Value does not match value Tip: When a DMS returns a simple flat value (e.g. Matter = "12345"), use {{ Matter }} directly. Only use dot notation (e.g. {{ matter.value }}) when the DMS returns nested data, such as when WithAdditionalData is enabled. "Provider" properties This property is used to set the current DMS system that is being used. There are three options: iManage: set to this option when iManage integration is required NetDocuments: set to this option when netDocs integration is required None: (default) set to this option when no DMS integration is required { "DocumentManagement": { "Provider": "NetDocuments", } } Vanilla iManage Net Docs Content for Vanilla... The iManage integration requires the Kutana+Brochet iManage Office Extension to be installed. iManage Office Extension There are two Work 10 iManage version: On Prem - Kutana+Brochet will provide the client with a zip file for them to install into their iManage Control Center iCloud - the client will enable the Kutana+Brochet ??? application in the iManage iCloud control center Installation requirements for the iManage Office Extension The Office Extension acquires the Keygen license status from the Word COMS add-in. It needs a valid Keygen license for any code to run. For the Office Extension to find the Kutana+Brochet COMs Addin it needs to know the name of the add-in. The default name it looks for is BrochetWordMainAddin (this was the name of the old Word Add-in). The new Word Add-in have been renamed to KutanaBrochet.WordAddIn and therefore the following registry key needs to be set with this new name: Computer\HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Brochet\Word\AddinName value = KutanaBrochet.WordAddIn NB: This key is not being set by the Word msi install and needs to be set manually as part of the installation. Configuration file location The configuration files (DesktopExtensionConfig.json) can be saved into several different locations. The K+B iManage Office Extension will look for these files in the following places, in the order they are listed: Location defined by a Registry setting Key: Computer\HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Brochet\iManageDesktopExtension Or Key: Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Brochet\iManageDesktopExtension Value: ConfigPath {needs to hold the full path and file name – the filename can be changed from the default file name} or Value: ConfigFolder {Path ONLY as a string – the file name must be the default file name DesktopExtensionConfig.json} NB: First read from HKEY_CURRENT_USER then HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE. NB: First version (version 36) of the iCloud K+B iManage Office Extension ONLY looks in the Registry for the configuration file ie it doesn't look for the file in the alternative location. NB: First version (version 36) of the iCloud K+B iManage Office Extension Also will not expand %appdata% or %username%. NB: Unlike the NetDocs integration the configuration for mapping the iManage data back into the Word document is done in this separate configuration file. DMSKeyNames The following DMSKEYNames can be used inf the "PropertyMappings" properties for saving into the Word document. Document Profile information profileInfo.identifier - Document number profileInfo.cabinet - profileInfo.version - Document version profileInfo.filename - Document file name profileInfo.lastModified - Date the document was last modified profileInfo.createdBy - Full name of the typist System information The other properties depend on how NetDocs is setup as will vary between different clients. View the New Document dialog box to look at the fields and values that need to be entered: On the left part of the screen are the list of fields these names can be used. Each has two elements .value and .description. Example: author.value eg EKT author.description eg Emma Thompson
