features/insert-snippet.html.title: Insert Snippet - User Guide | User Docs
features/insert-snippet.html.summary: Insert Snippet The Insert Snippet menu lets you insert the content of a pre-configured Word document into your active document at the current cursor position. This is useful for inserting standard clauses, boilerplate paragraphs, signature blocks, or any reusable content. How It Works Place your cursor where you want the content inserted Click the Insert Snippet drop-down button on the ribbon The add-in discovers snippet files from your configured folder(s) A menu appears listing available snippets, optionally grouped by folder Click a snippet name to insert its content at the cursor position NB: Unlike New Document and Starter Document, Insert Snippet does not create a new document. It inserts content into the currently active document. Ribbon.xml <dynamicMenu id="insertSnippetMenu" label="Insert Snippet" size="large" image="NewDocument" getVisible="GetVisibleByTag" getEnabled="GetEnabledByTag" getContent="GetSnippetDocuments" tag="insertSnippets" /> NB: The id attribute must match a key in the FileProviderConfiguration.InsertSnippet configuration section. The optional getVisible and getEnabled attributes use Ribbon Item Rules to control whether the menu appears and whether it is clickable. When using the ByTag variants, the tag attribute value is matched against rules instead of the id. If omitted, the menu is always visible and enabled. Configuration The Insert Snippet menu is configured within the FileProviderConfiguration section of your JSON configuration file. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "FileProviderConfiguration": { "InsertSnippet": { "insertSnippetMenu": { "Source": { "Location": "%Workgroup%", "MaxDepth": 2, "Extensions": [".docx", ".docm"] }, "Style": { "Title": "Snippets", "Type": "Flat", "RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames": true, "RemoveLeadingNumbersFromContainerNames": true, "SeparateByContainer": true } } } } } NB: The key "insertSnippetMenu" must match the id attribute on the <dynamicMenu> element in your Ribbon.xml. Source Properties Property Required Default Description Location No %Workgroup% Root folder path. Supports %Workgroup% and %User% placeholders MaxDepth No 0 Subdirectory depth to search. 0 = root only Extensions No [".docx", ".docm"] File extensions to include Prefixes No [] Only show files with these name prefixes. Empty = show all ExcludedContainers No [] Folder names to skip during discovery Style Properties Property Required Default Description Title No none Optional header text shown at the top of the menu Type No Flat Flat (single list with separators) or Group (nested submenus) RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames No true Strip numeric prefixes from file names RemoveLeadingNumbersFromContainerNames No true Strip numeric prefixes from folder names SeparateByContainer No true In Flat mode, show folder name separators between groups Creating Snippet Files Snippet files are standard Word documents (.docx or .docm) containing the content you want to insert. Best Practices One snippet per file: Each file should contain a single reusable block of content Use styles: Format content using your organisation's styles so it integrates cleanly with the target document Avoid headers/footers: Only the document body content is inserted Use numeric prefixes for ordering: 01_Signature Block.docx, 02_Confidentiality Clause.docx Folder Organisation Organise snippets into subfolders by category: Snippets/ 01_Clauses/ Confidentiality.docx Non-Compete.docx 02_Blocks/ Signature Block.docx Address Block.docx 03_Standard Text/ Disclaimer.docx Copyright Notice.docx With MaxDepth set to 1 or higher and SeparateByContainer enabled, the menu will group items by folder with separator headers. Display Styles Flat Items appear as a single list. When SeparateByContainer is true, items from different folders are visually separated with a folder name header. Group Items are organised into submenus by folder (maximum 2 levels deep). Folders with only a single subfolder and no files are compacted into one level. Troubleshooting Menu is empty Problem: The Insert Snippet drop-down shows no items. Solutions: Verify the Location path exists and is accessible Check that snippet files have the correct extensions (.docx or .docm) Ensure MaxDepth is set high enough to reach your snippet files Confirm the configuration key matches the ribbon button id Content not inserted Problem: Clicking a snippet does nothing. Solutions: Ensure you have an active document open - Insert Snippet requires a document to insert into Check that the snippet file still exists at its original location Verify that the add-in has a valid licence with the WordStandardTools entitlement Formatting looks wrong after insert Problem: Inserted content does not match the target document's formatting. Solutions: Ensure the snippet file uses named styles rather than direct formatting Check that the target document has matching style definitions Consider using the Clear Text Formatting utility after insertion if needed See Also New Document Starter Document Ribbon Item Rules Scripts Developer Guide

features/new-document.html.title: New Document - User Guide | User Docs
features/new-document.html.summary: New Document The New Document menu lets you create a new Word document based on an organisation template (.dotx or .dotm). Rather than navigating to a template folder manually, you select from a dynamically populated drop-down menu on the ribbon. How It Works Click the New Document drop-down button on the ribbon The add-in discovers template files from your configured template folder(s) A menu appears listing all available templates, optionally grouped by folder Click a template name to create a new document based on that template If your organisation uses a Document Management System (DMS), a pre-profile dialog may appear after the document is created, allowing you to save and categorise the document immediately. Ribbon.xml The New Document menu is a <dynamicMenu> element. Your organisation's Ribbon.xml should include an element like: <dynamicMenu id="newDocumentMenu" label="New Document" size="large" image="NewDocument" getVisible="GetVisibleById" getEnabled="GetEnabledById" getContent="GetNewDocuments" /> NB: The id attribute must match a key in the FileProviderConfiguration.NewDocument configuration section. Multiple New Document menus can be configured with different IDs to show different template sets. The optional getVisible and getEnabled attributes use Ribbon Item Rules to control whether the menu appears and whether it is clickable. If omitted, the menu is always visible and enabled. Configuration The New Document menu is configured within the FileProviderConfiguration section of your JSON configuration file. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "FileProviderConfiguration": { "NewDocument": { "newDocumentMenu": { "Source": { "Location": "%Workgroup%", "MaxDepth": 2, "Extensions": [".dotx", ".dotm"] }, "Style": { "Title": "Templates", "Type": "Flat", "RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames": true, "RemoveLeadingNumbersFromContainerNames": true, "SeparateByContainer": true } } } } } NB: The key "newDocumentMenu" must match the id attribute on the <dynamicMenu> element in your Ribbon.xml. Source Properties Property Required Default Description Location No %Workgroup% Root folder path. Supports %Workgroup% and %User% placeholders MaxDepth No 0 Subdirectory depth to search. 0 = root only Extensions No [".dotx", ".dotm"] File extensions to include Prefixes No [] Only show files with these name prefixes. Empty = show all ExcludedContainers No [] Folder names to skip during discovery Style Properties Property Required Default Description Title No none Optional header text shown at the top of the menu Type No Flat Flat (single list with separators) or Group (nested submenus) RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames No true Strip numeric prefixes (e.g. 01_Letter becomes Letter) RemoveLeadingNumbersFromContainerNames No true Strip numeric prefixes from folder names SeparateByContainer No true In Flat mode, show folder name separators between groups Path Placeholders %Workgroup% - resolves to the shared workgroup template path (typically set via Group Policy or installer) %User% - resolves to the user's personal template path Display Styles Flat Items appear as a single list. When SeparateByContainer is true, items from different folders are visually separated with a folder name header. Group Items are organised into submenus by folder. Each folder becomes a submenu (maximum 2 levels deep). Folders with only a single subfolder and no files are compacted into one level (e.g. Corporate / Letters instead of a Corporate submenu containing a Letters submenu). Template Organisation File Naming Templates can use numeric prefixes to control sort order: 01_Letter.dotx, 02_Memo.dotx When RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames is true, the prefix is stripped from the display name Supported prefix formats: 01_, 01-, 01., 01 , or just 01 Folder Structure Organise templates in subfolders for logical grouping Set MaxDepth to control how deep the add-in searches Use ExcludedContainers to hide folders like Archive or Deprecated Troubleshooting Menu is empty Problem: The New Document drop-down shows no items. Solutions: Verify the Location path exists and is accessible Check that template files have the correct extensions (.dotx or .dotm) Ensure MaxDepth is set high enough to reach your template files Confirm the configuration key matches the ribbon button id Templates not appearing from subfolders Problem: Templates in subfolders are not shown. Solutions: Increase the MaxDepth value (default is 0 which only searches the root folder) Check that the subfolder is not listed in ExcludedContainers See Also Starter Document Insert Snippet Ribbon Item Rules DMS Integration Developer Guide

features/ribbon-item-rules.html.title: Ribbon Item Rules - User Guide | User Docs
features/ribbon-item-rules.html.summary: Ribbon Item Rules Ribbon Item Rules let you control which ribbon buttons, menus, and groups are visible or enabled. Rules are evaluated dynamically — when conditions change (e.g. a document is opened or a licence is activated), the ribbon updates automatically. How It Works Each ribbon element has an id (or tag) attribute in Ribbon.xml The add-in matches that value against configured RibbonItemRules patterns If a matching rule is found, the rule's VisibleWhen and EnabledWhen values determine the item's state If no rule matches, the item defaults to visible and disabled Configuration Ribbon Item Rules are configured in the RulesConfiguration section of your JSON configuration file. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "RulesConfiguration": { "RibbonItemRules": [ { "Pattern": "newDocumentMenu", "VisibleWhen": "Always", "EnabledWhen": "DocumentMenusEnabled" }, { "Pattern": "starterDocument*", "VisibleWhen": "Always", "EnabledWhen": "DocumentMenusEnabled" }, { "Pattern": "*", "VisibleWhen": "Always", "EnabledWhen": "Always" } ] } } Rule Properties Property Required Default Description Pattern Yes — Glob pattern matched against the ribbon item's id or tag EnabledWhen No Always Workflow name determining when the item is enabled VisibleWhen No Always Workflow name determining when the item is visible Pattern Matching Patterns use glob syntax: Pattern Matches newDocumentMenu Exact match only starter* Any id starting with "starter" *Snippet* Any id containing "Snippet" * All ribbon items Rules are evaluated in order — the first matching rule wins. Place specific patterns before broad ones. VisibleWhen and EnabledWhen Values Value Meaning Always Always visible / enabled (this is the default) (empty or omitted) Same as Always (workflow name) Delegates to a RulesConfiguration.Workflows rule for dynamic evaluation When using a workflow name, the corresponding workflow must be defined in the Workflows section of RulesConfiguration. See Script Configuration for details on configuring workflows. Ribbon.xml Callbacks To use Ribbon Item Rules, add getVisible and getEnabled attributes to your ribbon elements: By ID (recommended for most elements) <dynamicMenu id="newDocumentMenu" getVisible="GetVisibleById" getEnabled="GetEnabledById" getContent="GetNewDocuments" /> The id attribute value is matched against rule patterns. NB: The getVisible="GetVisibleById" will also work on a group (do not include getEnabled="GetEnabledById" as this is not relvant for a group) By Tag (for shared rules across elements) <dynamicMenu id="insertSnippetMenu" getVisible="GetVisibleByTag" getEnabled="GetEnabledByTag" getContent="GetSnippetDocuments" tag="snippets" /> The tag attribute value is matched against rule patterns. This is useful when multiple ribbon elements should share the same visibility/enabled logic. Available Callbacks Callback Matches Against Default (no rule) GetVisibleById control.Id visible GetEnabledById control.Id disabled GetVisibleByTag control.Tag visible GetEnabledByTag control.Tag disabled Default Behaviour When no matching rule is found for a ribbon item: Visibility defaults to visible — items do not disappear unexpectedly Enabled defaults to disabled — items require an explicit rule to become clickable This is intentional: it is safer for an item to appear greyed-out than to silently vanish from the ribbon. Comparison with Script Button Rules Script buttons (using onAction="RunScript") have their own visibility/enabled system via ScriptConfiguration.Mappings: Ribbon Item Rules Script Mappings Callbacks GetVisibleById / GetEnabledById GetVisibleByScript / GetEnabledByScript Configuration RulesConfiguration.RibbonItemRules ScriptConfiguration.Mappings Pattern matching Glob patterns (e.g. starter*) Exact id match only Use case Dynamic menus, groups, non-script buttons Script-driven buttons Both systems reference the same RulesConfiguration.Workflows for conditional logic. Choose Ribbon Item Rules for menus and groups; use Script Mappings for buttons that run scripts. Examples Hide a menu entirely { "RibbonItemRules": [ { "Pattern": "starterDocumentMenu", "VisibleWhen": "NeverVisible" } ] } Where NeverVisible is a workflow that always evaluates to false. Enable menus only when a document is open { "RibbonItemRules": [ { "Pattern": "newDocumentMenu", "EnabledWhen": "DocumentIsOpen" }, { "Pattern": "starterDocumentMenu", "EnabledWhen": "DocumentIsOpen" } ] } Catch-all rule for all ribbon items { "RibbonItemRules": [ { "Pattern": "newDocumentMenu", "EnabledWhen": "SpecialCondition" }, { "Pattern": "*", "VisibleWhen": "Always", "EnabledWhen": "Always" } ] } The catch-all * rule ensures any item without a specific rule is visible and enabled. Place it last. Troubleshooting Menu is visible but greyed out Problem: A ribbon item appears but cannot be clicked. Solutions: Check that the item has an EnabledWhen rule configured (the default is disabled when no rule matches) Verify the referenced workflow exists in RulesConfiguration.Workflows Use "EnabledWhen": "Always" to make the item always enabled Menu has disappeared Problem: A ribbon item is not shown on the ribbon. Solutions: Check that the item's VisibleWhen rule does not evaluate to false Verify the Pattern matches the element's id (or tag if using ByTag callbacks) Confirm the RulesConfiguration JSON is valid and loaded correctly Remember that rules are evaluated in order — an earlier catch-all pattern may be matching first Rules not updating Problem: Changing conditions (e.g. opening a document) does not update the ribbon. Solutions: The ribbon refreshes automatically when the rules configuration changes via hot-reload If the issue persists, click the Refresh button (if available) or restart Word See Also New Document Starter Document Insert Snippet Script Configuration Developer Guide

features/scripts.html.title: Scripts - User Guide | User Docs
features/scripts.html.summary: Scripts Scripts allow you to automate multiple document editing operations with a single button click. Each script contains a sequence of commands that execute in order, helping you apply consistent formatting and maintain document standards efficiently. What are Scripts? Scripts are configurable automation tools that combine multiple editing commands into a single action. Rather than manually applying several formatting changes, you can create a script that applies all changes automatically to your selected text or entire document. Benefits Time-Saving: Execute multiple formatting operations with one click Consistency: Ensure uniform formatting across documents Reduced Errors: Eliminate manual formatting mistakes Customisable: Configure scripts to match your organisation's standards Reusable: Create once, use repeatedly across documents How is a Script activated? A Script can be activated via a button on the ribbon. Example ribbon button XML: <button id="OperativeLevel1Heading" image="Level 1 Heading" onAction="RunScript" label="Level 1 Heading" /> Notice that the onAction="RunScript" is being used. This is the command that is being run which will find the correct script to run using the button id="OperativeLevel1Heading". There is a JSON element ScriptConfiguration that needs to be set with the button mapping and the script instructions. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "ScriptConfiguration": { "Mappings": { "OperativeLevel1Heading": { "Script": "StyleLevel1Heading", "EnabledWhen": "OperativeGroupFullVisible", "VisibleWhen": "OperativeGroupFullVisible" } }, "Scripts": { "StyleLevel1Heading": { "Comment": "Applies Level 1 Heading Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Level 1 Heading" } } ] } } } } NB: The "Mappings": element is descriped in more details in ????? NB: The Mappings script name (text) in the line "Script": "StyleLevel1Heading", needs have a corresponding Scripts element with a matching name "StyleLevel1Heading": {. Creating a Script element A Script element is made up of three parts; a name and then two elements: Comment and Commands. Name - this needs to be unique and matches the Script name in the Mappings section of the JSON file "Comment": this element is only used for information by the person editting the JSON file as comments are not possible in JSON files. "Commands": this element is an array of Script commands that are run in sequence. There must be at least one script command eg to apply a style or a sequence of commands to perform a sequence of commands. "Scripts": { "StyleLevel1Heading": { "Comment": "Applies Level 1 Heading Style", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "StyleName": "Level 1 Heading" } } ] } "Commands" elements A Commands element is made up of three parts; a name and then two elements: Comment and Commands. "$type" - this is the name of the script command. There are 12 types of command - Mandatory. "Range": - this holds the range that the script is going to affect within the current Word document. Some of the Command types do not require a Range option (Mandatory for some types). "Options": this element holds the different options available for the Command type. There is always at least one Options for all script commands. Available Script Commands types Scripts support 13 types of commands that can be combined in any sequence: Formatting Commands "$type" 1. Apply Font Formatting ("$type": "ApplyFontFormatting") Applies character-level formatting to text, including: Bold, italic, underline, doublestrikethrough, strikethrough CharacterCase Font colour and colour index All caps, small caps Superscript and subscript Text position Hidden text 2. Apply Paragraph Formatting ("$type": "ApplyParagraphFormatting") Applies paragraph-level formatting, including: Alignment (left, right, centre, justify) Indentation (left, right, first line) Line spacing and spacing before/after KeepWithNext, KeepWithNext, PageBreakBefore OutlineLevel, NoLineNumber Tab stops WindowControl 3. Apply Style ("$type": "ApplyStyle") Applies a named paragraph style to the selected text or range. NB: There are three types of Style: Paragraph, Character and Linked (Paragraph and Character) 4. Clear manually applied font formatting ("$type": "ClearFontFormatting") Clears all manually applied font formatting to the selection or range - equivalent to Ctrl+Spacebar 5. Clear manually applied paragraph formatting ("$type": "ClearParagraphFormatting") Clears all manually applied paragraph formatting to the selection or range. Text Manipulation Commands 6. Delete Characters ("$type": "DeleteCharacters") Removes specified characters from the text. Useful for cleaning up unwanted characters or formatting marks. 7. Delete Words ("$type": "DeleteWords") Removes specified characters from the text. Useful for cleaning up unwanted characters or formatting marks. 8. Enforce Case ("$type": "EnforceCase") Converts text to a specific case: Uppercase (ALL CAPS) Lowercase (all lowercase) Title Case (First Letter Capital) Sentence case (First letter of sentence) 9. Insert Field ("$type": "InsertField") Inserts a Word field (such as page numbers, dates, or document properties) at the specified location. 10. Insert Word ("$type": "InsertWord") Inserts specified text at the cursor position or within a range. Can optionally prevent duplicate insertions. Custom Document Property Manipulation Commands 11. Set the value of a Custom Document Property ("$type": "SetCustomProperty") Used to set the value of a Custom Document Property. These can be used to show or hide buttons for groups on the ribbon. Use in tandem with UnsetCustomProperty 12. Delete a Custom Document Property ("$type": "DeleteCustomProperty") Used to unset the value of a Custom Document Property. These can be used to show or hide buttons for groups on the ribbon. Use in tandem with SetCustomProperty VBA Macro Commands 13. Run VBA Macro ("$type": "RunMacro") Executes an existing VBA macro by name with optional parameters. This allows you to integrate existing VBA automation with your script configurations. Use Cases: Execute legacy VBA macros from ribbon buttons Run document-specific macros stored in templates Trigger complex automation that's already implemented in VBA ??. Delete Text / Replace Text???? (Not yet available) Removes specified characters from the text. Useful for cleaning up unwanted characters or formatting marks. Using Scripts Scripts appear as buttons in your Word ribbon. The visibility and availability of scripts are controlled by configuration and can be customised per user or organisation. Executing a Script Select text (if the script applies to a selection) or position your cursor Click the script button in the ribbon The script executes all commands in sequence Changes are applied to your document Tip Scripts respect Word's undo stack. If you need to reverse script changes, press Ctrl+Z immediately after execution. Script Behaviour Sequential Execution: Commands execute in the order configured Range Application: Each command can target different ranges: Entire document ("CommandRange": "EntireDocument") - "THIS DOES NOT EXIST IN THE SCHEMEA AT THE MOMENT SO NOT AVAILABLE" Current selection ("CommandRange": "Selection") All paragraphs of selection ("CommandRange": "Paragraphs") First paragraph of selection ("CommandRange": "FirstParagraph") Last paragraph of selection ("CommandRange": "LastParagraph") SubRange Application: the following Range options can also have a SubRange ("CommandRange": "Selection") and : Selection ("CommandRange": "Selection") with sub-ranges (first word, last word, all words, first sentence, last sentence, all sentences) Current selection ("CommandRange": "Selection") Error Handling: If a command fails, script execution stops, and you receive an error message Configuration Scripts are configured through JSON files stored in your application data directory: Shared Configuration: %appdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config User Configuration: %localappdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config JSON Schema See CoreConfiguration.schema.json for the full configuration schema. Tip Add a $schema property for IntelliSense and validation in your JSON editor: { "$schema": "path/to/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "Scripts": { ... } } Script Structure Each script requires: Name: Unique identifier for the script Description: Plain-language explanation of what the script does Commands: List of operations to perform Mapping: Controls visibility and availability in the ribbon Configuration Example { "Scripts": { "FormatLegalDocument": { "Comment": "Applies standard legal document formatting", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "CommandRange": "AllParagraphs", "StyleName": "Legal Body Text" }, { "$type": "ApplyFontFormatting", "CommandRange": "Selection", "SubRange": "All", "Bold": 0, "Italic": 0 }, { "$type": "DeleteCharacters", "CommandRange": "EntireDocument", "SubRange": "All", "Characters": " " } ] } }, "Mappings": { "formatLegalDoc": { "Script": "FormatLegalDocument", "EnabledWhen": "Always", "VisibleWhen": "Always" } } } Configuration Options EnabledWhen Always: Script is always available Custom rules expression (advanced) VisibleWhen Always: Script button always visible in ribbon Custom rules expression (advanced) CommandRange Options (Is the attribute mandatory ??? Check with Phill EntireDocument: Applies to whole document ("CommandRange": "EntireDocument") Selection: Applies to currently selected text ("CommandRange": "Selection") Paragraphs: Applies to every paragraph in the selection ("CommandRange": "Paragraphs") FirstParagraph: Applies to first paragraph only in the selection ("CommandRange": "FirstParagraph") LastParagraph: Applies to last paragraph only in the selection ("CommandRange": "LastParagraph") SubRange Options (for granular control) All: Entire range FirstWord: First word only LastWord: Last word only Available on InsertText only Start: First word only End: Last word only Command Types Reference 1. ApplyFontFormatting Mandatory Properties: Ranges: $type: EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, Paragraphs, Selection SubRange: All, FirstWord, LastWord Options: There needs to be at least one option for the Script to do anything NB: EntireDocument applies to the Main story in the document - it dose not include text in a textbox in the main story NB: The ("$type": "ClearFontFormatting") can be used to remove all manual formatting prior to running font formatting commands "Comment": "Testing Applying Bold", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyFontFormatting", "Range": { "$type": "Selection", "SubRange": "All" }, "Options": { "Bold": "True" } } ] Available Options: at least one must be used for the script to do anything: Bold, Italic: False, Toggle, True CharacterCase: FullWidth, HalfWidth, LowerCase, NextCase, TitleSentence, TitleWord, ToggleCase, UpperCase AllCaps, SmallCaps: False, Toggle, True Underline: None, Single, Double, Dotted, etc. Colour: Text string that can either be the RGB colour value enters as a hex value e.g. #1234AB. (Originally RGB values were permitted e.g. RGB(0,0,255) but these are no longer supported.) ColourIndex: 0 - Not sure how this is used ????? Hidden: False, Toggle, True Position: Raise/lower text (in points) Subscript, Superscript: False, Toggle, True StrikeThrough, DoubleStrikeThrough: False, Toggle, True 2. ApplyParagraphFormatting Mandatory Properties: Ranges: $type: EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, Paragraphs, Selection Options: There needs to be at least one option for the Script to do anything NB: There is no SubType option in the Range for ApplyParagraphFormatting as it applies to whole paragraphs. "Comment": "Testing removing space after", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyParagraphFormatting", "Range": { "$type": "Selection" }, "Options": { "SpaceAfter": 0 } } ] Available Options at least one must be used for the script to do anything: Alignment: Center, Justified, Left, Right BaseLineAlignment: Baseline, Center, FarEast50, Top CharacterUnitFirstLineIndent, CharacterUnitLeftIndent, CharacterUnitRightIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) FirstLineIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) KeepTogether, KeepWithNext: False, True, Undefined LeftIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) LineSpacing: 0 value in points (always +ve?) LineSpacingRule: AtLeast, Double, Exactly, Multiple, OnePointFive, Single LineUnitAfter, LineUnitBefore: 0 value in points (always +ve?) MirrorIndents: False, True, Undefined NoLineNumber: False, True, Undefined OutlineLevel: BodyText, Level1, Level2, . . . Level9 PageBreakBefore: False, True, Undefined RightIndent: 0 value in points (always +ve?) SpaceAfter, SpaceBefore: 0 value in points (always +ve?) WidowControl: False, True, Undefined 3. Apply Selection Formatting $type: ApplySelectionFormatting, CommandRanges: EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, Paragraphs, Selection SubRange: Not used - the default option is All StyleName:

features/square-brackets.html.title: Square Brackets - User Guide | User Docs
features/square-brackets.html.summary: Square Brackets The Square Brackets group provides tools for working with bracket pairs [...] in legal documents. These tools help you quickly navigate between placeholders, add bracketed sections, and manage bracketed content during document drafting and review. Square brackets are commonly used in legal documents to mark: Optional clauses or alternative text Placeholder content to be filled in Sections requiring review or client input Draft content that may be removed Features The Square Brackets group includes six ribbon buttons organised into three functional areas: Navigation Find Next - Jump to the next square bracket pair Find Previous - Jump to the previous square bracket pair Adding Brackets Add Brackets - Wrap the selected text in square brackets Insert Blob - Insert a special marker between brackets (e.g., [X] or [●]) Removing Brackets Delete Brackets - Remove the brackets but keep the content Delete All - Remove both the brackets and their content How It Works Find Next / Find Previous Navigate through all square bracket pairs in your document sequentially. Click Find Next or Find Previous on the ribbon The add-in locates the next/previous bracket pair from your current cursor position The bracket pair is highlighted with the content selected At the end of the document, you'll be prompted to restart from the beginning (or vice versa) NB: If no bracket pairs exist in the document, you'll see a notification message. Special handling for placeholder content controls: If your cursor is inside a placeholder content control that displays brackets, the find operation will treat it as a bracket pair This allows consistent navigation across both text brackets and content control placeholders Add Brackets Wrap your selected text in square brackets. Select the text you want to bracket Click Add Brackets on the ribbon Square brackets are added around your selection: Hello World → [Hello World] The selection is expanded to include the newly added brackets NB: Works on any text selection, including: Single words Multiple paragraphs Text that already contains brackets (creates nested brackets: [already [bracketed]]) Empty selection (inserts [] at cursor position) Insert Blob Insert a special marker between square brackets, commonly used to mark a position for later completion. Select the text you want to replace (or click where you want to insert) Click Insert Blob on the ribbon The selection is replaced with your configured blob text between brackets Default behaviour: With no configuration: Inserts [] with a Wingdings character With configured text: Inserts [X] (or your configured marker) NB: The blob character typically uses special formatting (e.g., Wingdings font) to create a visual marker like [●] that stands out in the document. Delete Brackets Remove the square brackets but preserve the content inside. Select a bracket pair (must include both [ and ]) Click Delete Brackets on the ribbon The brackets are removed: [Hello World] → Hello World The cursor is positioned where the opening bracket was Requirements: Selection must start with [ and end with ] If the selection is invalid, you'll see a notification message The document remains unchanged if selection criteria aren't met Delete All Remove both the square brackets and their content. Select a bracket pair (must include both [ and ]) Click Delete All on the ribbon The entire bracketed section is removed: Hello [World] Test → Hello Test The cursor is positioned where the opening bracket was Requirements: Selection must start with [ and end with ] If the selection is invalid, you'll see a notification message The document remains unchanged if selection criteria aren't met Ribbon.xml <group id="SquareBracketsGroup" getVisible="GetVisibleById" label="[...]"> <box id="boxSB" boxStyle="horizontal"> <button id="FindPreviousSquareBrackets" onAction="FindPreviousSquareBrackets" label="Previous" showLabel="false" image="BracketsPrevious" screentip="Find Previous Square Brackets" supertip="Navigate to the previous pair of square brackets in the document." /> <button id="FindNextSquareBrackets" onAction="FindNextSquareBrackets" label="Next" showLabel="false" image="BracketsNext" screentip="Find Next Square Brackets" supertip="Navigate to the next pair of square brackets in the document." /> </box> <box id="boxAdd" boxStyle="horizontal"> <button id="AddSquareBrackets" onAction="AddSquareBrackets" label="Add" showLabel="false" image="BracketsPlus" screentip="Add Square Brackets" supertip="Add square brackets around the selected text." /> <button id="InsertBlob" onAction="InsertBlob" label="Blob" showLabel="false" image="Blob" screentip="Insert Blob" supertip="Insert a special marker between square brackets at the current position." /> </box> <box id="boxDelete" boxStyle="horizontal"> <button id="DeleteSquareBrackets" onAction="DeleteSquareBrackets" label="Delete" showLabel="false" image="deleteBrackets" screentip="Delete Square Brackets" supertip="Remove the square brackets but keep the content inside." /> <button id="DeleteAllSquareBrackets" onAction="DeleteSquareBracketsIncludingContent" label="Delete All" showLabel="false" image="deleteAll" screentip="Delete Brackets and Content" supertip="Remove both the square brackets and their content." /> </box> </group> NB: The id attributes can be customised as needed. The onAction attribute values must match the method names in ProductRibbon.cs. The optional getVisible and getEnabled attributes use Ribbon Item Rules to control whether buttons appear and whether they are clickable. Configuration Square Brackets functionality can be customised via the SquareBracketsConfiguration section of your JSON configuration file. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "SquareBracketsConfiguration": { "Delimiter": { "Open": "[", "Close": "]" }, "Blob": { "Text": "X", "FontName": "Wingdings", "FontSize": 10 } } } Delimiter Properties Property Required Default Description Open No [ The opening delimiter character Close No ] The closing delimiter character NB: While you can customise the delimiter characters (e.g., to use curly braces {}), changing them affects all Square Brackets operations. Most legal workflows use the default square brackets. Blob Properties Property Required Default Description Text No Empty string The text to insert between brackets. If empty, uses Wingdings character FontName No Wingdings Font name for the blob character FontSize No 10 Font size for the blob character Common blob text values: X - Simple letter X marker Empty string - Uses Wingdings font for special character ● - Bullet point (if font supports it) TODO - Text reminder Schema See CoreConfiguration.schema.json for the full configuration schema. Add "$schema": "path/to/CoreConfiguration.schema.json" to your JSON file for IntelliSense and validation. Example Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Troubleshooting Find commands do nothing Problem: Clicking Find Next/Previous has no effect. Solutions: Verify square brackets exist in the document Check that you're using the correct bracket characters [ and ] Ensure the add-in has a valid licence Delete commands show "invalid selection" Problem: Delete Brackets or Delete All shows a notification about invalid selection. Solutions: Ensure your selection starts with [ and ends with ] Select the entire bracket pair, not just the content inside Avoid selecting text before the opening bracket or after the closing bracket Quick selection tip: Use Find Next/Previous to automatically select valid bracket pairs, then apply Delete operations. Insert Blob shows unexpected character Problem: Insert Blob creates a different character than expected. Solutions: Check your Blob.Text configuration setting Verify the Blob.FontName is installed on your system Try using a standard font (e.g., Arial) with explicit text (e.g., X) Add Brackets inserts at wrong position Problem: Add Brackets doesn't wrap the intended text. Solutions: Ensure you have text selected before clicking Add Brackets If inserting at a specific position, click to place your cursor first Verify no content controls or fields are interfering with the selection Use Cases Legal Drafting Scenario: Marking optional clauses during contract drafting. Select the optional clause text Click Add Brackets to mark it as [optional clause] Use Find Next/Previous during review to navigate between all optional sections Use Delete Brackets to keep finalised clauses or Delete All to remove rejected ones Document Review Scenario: Marking placeholders for client input. Place cursor where information is needed Click Insert Blob to add [X] or [●] as a visual marker Send to client with instructions to replace bracketed markers Use Find Next to quickly locate and verify all inputs upon return Template Preparation Scenario: Creating document templates with multiple placeholders. Draft the template with descriptive placeholder text Select each placeholder section and click Add Brackets Use Find Next/Previous to review all bracketed sections Save as a template for future use Document Finalisation Scenario: Cleaning up a document before final delivery. Use Find Next to locate all remaining square brackets For sections to keep: Use Delete Brackets to remove markers For sections to remove: Use Delete All to delete entirely Continue until no brackets remain See Also Content Controls - Alternative method for managing placeholders Ribbon Item Rules - Control button visibility based on document state Utilities - Other document editing tools Developer Guide - Technical implementation details

features/starter-document.html.title: Starter Document - User Guide | User Docs
features/starter-document.html.summary: Starter Document The Starter Document menu lets you create a new Word document based on an existing pre-filled document. Unlike the New Document menu which creates a blank document from a template, a starter document already contains content — headings, boilerplate text, tables, or other standard elements — giving you a head start. How It Works Click the Starter Document drop-down button on the ribbon The add-in discovers starter document files from your configured folder(s) A menu appears listing available starter documents, optionally grouped by folder Click a starter document name to create a new editable copy Word opens a new document with the starter document's content. The original starter document file is not modified. If your organisation uses a Document Management System (DMS), a pre-profile dialog may appear after the document is created. Starter Documents vs Templates New Document Starter Document Source file .dotx / .dotm template .docx / .docm / .dotx / .dotm document New document content Blank (styles only) Pre-filled with content Use case Starting from scratch with correct styles Starting with standard boilerplate Template attached The selected template Automatically resolved from the file Template Resolution Each starter document has an attached template embedded in its file metadata. When you create a document from a starter, the add-in: Reads the attached template name from the starter document file Looks up the matching template in your configured template location Creates the new document with both the starter content and the resolved template If the attached template cannot be found, the default template is used as a fallback. Ribbon.xml <dynamicMenu id="starterDocumentMenu" label="Starter Document" size="large" image="NewDocument" getVisible="GetVisibleById" getEnabled="GetEnabledById" getContent="GetStarterDocuments" /> NB: The id attribute must match a key in the FileProviderConfiguration.StarterDocument configuration section. The optional getVisible and getEnabled attributes use Ribbon Item Rules to control whether the menu appears and whether it is clickable. If omitted, the menu is always visible and enabled. Staleness Indicators Starter document menu items display a colour-coded icon to indicate how recently the file was last modified: Default (blue icon) - the file is current Amber icon - the file has not been updated for a configured number of days Red icon - the file has not been updated for a longer period The screentip for each item always shows the last modified date in short date format. This helps you identify starter documents that may contain outdated content and need review. Configuring Staleness Staleness thresholds are configured in the Style.Staleness section: "Style": { "Staleness": { "Thresholds": [ { "AfterDays": 30, "Icon": "PersonaStatusAway" }, { "AfterDays": 90, "Icon": "PersonaStatusBusy" } ] } } Property Description AfterDays Number of days since last modification before this threshold applies Icon The Office icon to display (e.g. PersonaStatusAway for amber, PersonaStatusBusy for red) Thresholds are evaluated in order; the last matching threshold wins. Configuration The Starter Document menu is configured within the FileProviderConfiguration section of your JSON configuration file. { "$schema": "https://kutana-brochet.github.io/schemas/7.0/CoreConfiguration.schema.json", "FileProviderConfiguration": { "StarterDocument": { "starterDocumentMenu": { "Source": { "Location": "%Workgroup%", "MaxDepth": 2, "Extensions": [".docx", ".docm", ".dotx", ".dotm"] }, "Style": { "Title": "Starters", "Type": "Flat", "RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames": true, "RemoveLeadingNumbersFromContainerNames": true, "SeparateByContainer": true, "Staleness": { "Thresholds": [ { "AfterDays": 30, "Icon": "PersonaStatusAway" }, { "AfterDays": 90, "Icon": "PersonaStatusBusy" } ] } } } } } } NB: The key "starterDocumentMenu" must match the id attribute on the <dynamicMenu> element in your Ribbon.xml. Source Properties Property Required Default Description Location No %Workgroup% Root folder path. Supports %Workgroup% and %User% placeholders MaxDepth No 0 Subdirectory depth to search. 0 = root only Extensions No [".docx", ".docm", ".dotx", ".dotm"] File extensions to include Prefixes No [] Only show files with these name prefixes. Empty = show all ExcludedContainers No [] Folder names to skip during discovery Style Properties Property Required Default Description Title No none Optional header text shown at the top of the menu Type No Flat Flat (single list with separators) or Group (nested submenus) RemoveLeadingNumbersFromTemplateNames No true Strip numeric prefixes from file names RemoveLeadingNumbersFromContainerNames No true Strip numeric prefixes from folder names SeparateByContainer No true In Flat mode, show folder name separators between groups Staleness No none Staleness threshold configuration (see above) Display Styles Flat Items appear as a single list. When SeparateByContainer is true, items from different folders are visually separated with a folder name header. Group Items are organised into submenus by folder (maximum 2 levels deep). Folders with only a single subfolder and no files are compacted into one level. Troubleshooting Menu is empty Problem: The Starter Document drop-down shows no items. Solutions: Verify the Location path exists and is accessible Check that files have the correct extensions (.docx, .docm, .dotx, or .dotm) Ensure MaxDepth is set high enough to reach your files Confirm the configuration key matches the ribbon button id Staleness icons not appearing Problem: All items show the default icon regardless of file age. Solutions: Check that Staleness.Thresholds is configured in the Style section Verify that AfterDays values are appropriate for your update cycle Confirm the Icon values are valid Office icon names Wrong template applied Problem: A document created from a starter has incorrect styles. Solutions: Open the starter document file and check its attached template via File > Info Ensure the attached template exists in your configured template location If the template cannot be resolved, the default template is used as a fallback See Also New Document Insert Snippet Ribbon Item Rules DMS Integration Developer Guide

features/utilities.html.title: Utilities | User Docs
features/utilities.html.summary: Utilities The Utilities group contains a set of tools designed to make document management and formatting more efficient and consistent. These tools help you maintain style standards, manage templates, and apply common settings with just a few clicks, ensuring that your documents remain professional and aligned with your organization's formatting rules. NB: This is a standard Word idMso command Toggle Paragraph Marks (idMso ParagraphMarks) Known as "Show/Hide ¶" in native Word, the Toggle Paragraph Marks feature in Word, often represented by the ¶ symbol, is used to show or hide non-printing characters in your document. These characters are invisible when you print or view the document normally but can affect spacing, layout, and formatting. What it shows: When you toggle it on, Word displays symbols for: Paragraph breaks (¶): Marks the end of a paragraph. Spaces (·): Small dots showing spaces between words. Tabs (→): Arrows showing where tab stops are used. Line breaks (↵): Indicates a manual line break without starting a new paragraph. Hidden text: Text formatted as hidden will also appear. Why it’s useful: Troubleshooting formatting issues: If spacing looks off or alignment isn’t right, these markers help you see exactly what’s happening. Editing complex documents: Shows where extra spaces, tabs, or paragraph marks might be affecting layout. Consistent formatting: Ensures that elements like tables, lists, and indents are structured correctly. see more at https://support.microsoft.com/en-gb/office/show-or-hide-tab-marks-in-word-84a53213-5d02-404a-b022-09cae1a3958b Ribbon.xml <toggleButton idMso="ParagraphMarks" /> Clear Text Formatting (Command ClearTextFormatting) The Clear Text Formatting tool removes all manual character-level formatting from the selected text. Note: This is equivalent to pressing the Control+Spacebar shortcut key on the keyboard - but there is no in-built ClearFontFormatting button in Word to add to the Quick Access Toolbar! This is useful when text has been copied from other sources or heavily edited and contains inconsistent font styling. Strips away character-specific formatting, such as: Font type, size, and colour Bold, italic, underline, strikethrough Highlighting and text effects Leaves the plain text content intact This is often used in conjuction with the Clear Paragraph Formatting tool. Note: this does not remove colour applied using the "Text Highlight Colour" tool - this is by design (by Microsoft), Word considers it a display option. Usage The tool only applies to the current selected text - if only part of a paragraph is selected then applies to the selected text ONLY. Ribbon.xml <button id="ClearTextFormatting" onAction="ClearTextFormatting" label="Clear Manual Text Formatting" showLabel="false" image="ClearTextMarking" screentip="Clear Manual Text Formatting" supertip="Clear additional manual text formatting. Any changes to the text other than that defined by the style will be removed." /> Note: that some older .xml files call a function RemoveTextFormatting - this will not work. Clear Paragraph Formatting (Command ClearParagraphFormatting) The Clear Paragraph Formatting tool removes all manual paragraph-level formatting from the selected text, restoring it to the formatting defined by the applied style. Note: This is equivalent to pressing the Control+Q shortcut key on the keyboard - but there is no in-built ClearParagraphFormatting button in Word to add to the Quick Access Toolbar! This is useful when text has inconsistent or unwanted formatting applied using the Paragraph Formatting options in Word. Strips away paragraph-specific formatting, such as: Indents (left, right, first line) Line spacing and spacing before/after paragraphs Alignment (left, right, center, justify) Tabs and paragraph-level borders/shading Keeps the text content and character formatting (like bold or italic) intact This is often used in conjuction with the Clear Text Formatting tool. Usage The tool only applies to the current selection - if only part of a paragraph is selected then applies to the whole paragraph as this is paragraph formatting. Ribbon.xml <button id="ClearParagraphFormatting" onAction="ClearParagraphFormatting" label="Clear Paragraph Formatting" showLabel="false" image="ClearParagraphMarking" screentip="Clear Manual Paragraph Formatting" supertip="Clear additional manual paragraph formatting. Any changes to the paragraph other than that defined by the style will be removed." /> Note: that some older .xml files call a function RemoveTextFormattingAll - this will not work. Toggle Navigation Pane (idMso DocumentMapReadingView) The Navigation pane is a built-in tool in Microsoft Word that helps you quickly browse, search, and reorganise the content of your document. It’s especially useful in large or structured documents with headings. Headings View Displays a list of all headings in the document, based on styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.) Lets you jump directly to any section by clicking the heading Supports drag-and-drop to reorder sections, which moves both the heading and its associated content Page Thumbnails View Shows small thumbnail previews of each page Helps you visually navigate to specific pages quickly Search (Results) View Lets you search for words or phrases in the document Displays a list of all results; clicking a result jumps to its location Usage The tool only applies to the current selected document. This command needs to be used on a per document basis. Ribbon.xml Note: The icon has been changed to the standard one we use in the ribbon <button idMso="DocumentMapReadingView" showLabel="false" imageMso="RmsNavigationBar" /> Toggle Table Grid Lines The View Table Gridlines feature in Microsoft Word lets you see the structure of tables that don’t have visible borders. It shows dotted lines where table cell boundaries are, making it easier to work with the layout even if no borders are applied. Ribbon.xml <toggleButton idMso="TableShowGridlines" showLabel="false" /> Toggle Print/Draft View Provides a convenient way to switch between Draft view and Print Layout view Draft View in Microsoft Word is a document view designed to help you focus on writing and editing text content without being distracted by complex layout elements. It prioritizes speed and simplicity over precise page appearance. It also clearly displays the Paragraph styles used. Print Layout view in Microsoft Word shows your document exactly as it will appear on the printed page. It is the default and most commonly used view, designed for working on the visual formatting and layout of your document. Ribbon.xml <button id="TogglePrintDraft" onAction="TogglePrintDraftView" label="Draft/Print view" showLabel="false" imageMso="Zoom100" screentip="Toggle Print/Draft View" supertip="Toggle between Print View and Draft View." /> Delete Double Paragraphs Removes unnecessary empty paragraphs (blank lines) from your document, improving structure and readability. By doing so, paragraph styles can function as intended, ensuring consistent formatting throughout the document in line with company branding. WHY THIS SHOULD BE "EXTRA" RETURNS ALC - Also add command details Usage The tool first processes the selected text and then prompts you to check the rest of the document. If it reaches the end, it will ask whether you want to start from the beginning. After completing the operation, it reports the total number of replacements made. Ribbon.xml <button id="deleteDoubleParagraphsButton" onAction="DeleteDoubleParagraphs" screentip="Delete Double Paragraphs" label="Delete Double Paragraphs" showLabel="false" image="DoubleParaDelete" /> Delete Double Spaces Removes unnecessary spaces from your document, this ensures consistent spacing, improves readability and helps maintain a professional layout. Usage The tool first processes the selected text and then prompts you to check the rest of the document. If it reaches the end, it will ask whether you want to start from the beginning. After completing the operation, it reports the total number of replacements made. Ribbon.xml <button id="deleteDoubleSpacesButton" onAction="DeleteDoubleSpaces" label="Delete Double Spaces" showLabel="false" screentip="Delete Double Spaces" image="DeleteDoubleSpaces" /> Smart Quotes The Smart Quotes tool helps you manage quotation marks consistently throughout your document. Toggle Smart Quotes A toggle button to turn Word’s Smart Quotes feature on or off globally. When enabled, Word automatically converts straight quotes (") to typographic (curly) quotes (“”) as you type. Convert to Smart Quotes Converts all quotation marks in the selected text to Smart Quotes. Useful for cleaning up pasted text or documents created with straight quotes. Convert to Straight Quotes Converts all quotation marks in the selected text to straight quotes ("). Useful for coding, data entry, or formatting for plain text environments. Usage The tool first processes the selected text and then prompts you to check the rest of the document. If it reaches the end, it will ask whether you want to start from the beginning. After completing the operation, it reports the total number of replacements made. Ribbon.xml <splitButton id="SmartQuotesSplit" size="normal" showLabel="false"> <toggleButton id="SmartQuotesToggle" onAction="ToggleSmartQuotes" label="Smart Quotes" showLabel="false" image="Quotes" screentip="Toggle Smart Quotes" supertip="Toggle the use of smart quotes in the document." /> <menu id="SmartQuotesMenu"> <button id="SmartQuotes" onAction="SmartQuotes" label="Smart Quotes" showLabel="false" screentip="Switch from straight quotes to Smart quotes." image="CurlyQuotes" /> <button id="StraightQuotes" onAction="StraightQuotes" label="Straight Quotes" showLabel="false" screentip="Switch from Smart quotes to Straight quotes." image="StraightQuotes" /> </menu> </splitButton> Reset Styles The Reset Styles tool in restores all styles in your document to match the original styles defined in the template it was based on. This ensures consistent formatting and helps maintain adherence to company or template standards. Ribbon.xml <button id="ResetStyles" onAction="ResetStyles" label="Reset Styles" showLabel="false" visible="true" imageMso="StylesDialogClassic" screentip="Reset Paragraph Styles to default settings" /> AttachTemplate The Attach Template tool lets you review and modify the template attached to the current document. It provides options to redefine the default template and optionally reset the document’s styles to match that template. Example dialog box Currently attached template: - displays the name of the template that is currently attached the to the selected document. Default template to attach: - the contents (the template name) of the custom document property TemplatePropertyName is displayed. The property TemplatePropertyName contains the Custom Document Property name that this information is saved into if not set then the default K+BTemplate is used. This setting is used by the command AttachTemplateOnOpen which checks the correct template is attached to the document on the DocumentOpen event. List of available templates: This list the available templates. See How the templates to be displayed are found below for the file location and the templates that are listed. The templates that are listed show three further bits of information: Version - this is picked up from the Custom Document properties of the document. This will use the following sequence of custom document properties read in the following order: K+BVersion, BrochetVersion, TemplafyVersion (need to double check that this name) Date - this is picked up from the Custom Document properties of the document. This will use the following sequence of custom document properties read in the following order: K+BDate, BrochetDate, TemplafyDate (need to double check that this name) Additional information - this is picked up from the Comments field on the document properties (NOTE: not K+BComments) Highlighted template in the List of avilable templates: Highlights the template name which is currently attached to the document (e.g., Normal.dotm). NOTE: Git# 225 - this request is to where the Version and Date is read: eg for Version reads in the following order and stops as soon as information is found:K+BVersion -> BrochetVersion -> TemplafyVersion. NOTE: Git# 227 - Request for a loading symbol when AttachedTemplates have been pressed and before the dialog box comes up. NOTE: Git# 230 - The documents attached template is highlighted in the list but is not visible is low down in the list - need to scroll the list to make it visible NOTE: Git# 231 - Using on a Word template - should not display an attached template - should not allow the Reset Styles option How the templates to be displayed are found See the Scheme below. The following options can be set SearchForTemplateName - default setting is Normal.dotm. This is the template name that is looked for in the following locations: User Templates then Workgroup Template. Once found this is the templates location that is used to source all the templates listed in the listbox on the dialog box. If the template name is not found in either of the subdirectories then the default setting is used (Normal.dotm) Which templates are displayed in the listbox See the Scheme below. The following options can be set IncludeFilter - default setting is "^.*\.dot[mx]\(". This uses regular expressions to determine the templates to be listed. Example: "^Test.*\\.dot[mx]\) will only list templates which start with the text "Test" eg Testing 1.dotx, Testing 2.dotx. ExcludeFilter - default setting is "". This uses regular expressions to determine the templates to be excluded from the list. All temporary Word template files (ones that start with ~ are excluded as default). Key User Features User selects one template that is to be attached to the selected document (highlighted in the list) User ticks/unticks the **Reset default template name** option User ticks/unticks the **Reset document styles** option ON PRESSING OK **Always on pressing OK** sets the selected document to have the selected template as the attached template. **Reset default template name** if ticked saves the selected template name into the custom document property defined by the property "TemplatePropertyName". The default setting is "K+BTemplate". **Reset document styles** if ticked resets all styles in the document to match the attached template. Default config JSON "AttachTemplateConfiguration": { "SearchForTemplateName": "Normal.dotm", "IncludeFilter": "^.*\\.dot[mx]$", "ExcludeFilter": "", "TemplatePropertyName": "K+BTemplate" } PROPERTY OPTIONS "SearchForTemplateName" - this is the name of the template the code searches for to determine which templates location to use eg User Templates or Workgroup Templates. "IncludeFilter" - this is a regular expression that will list the templates to be displayed in the listbox. "ExcludeFilter" - this is a regular expression that will list the templates NOT to be displayed in the listbox. "TemplatePropertyName" - this is the name of the custom document property that holds the default template name. This information is used on the ??? option which makes sure the correct template is attached to the document. This is useful for Templafy clients or when a document is sent outside the firm and is returned with the document attached to Normal.dotm. Ribbon.xml <button id="AttachTemplate" onAction="AttachTemplate" label="Check Template" showLabel="false" visible="true" screentip="Check Template attached to Current Document" supertip="Displays a dialog box showing the Word template that is currently attached to the document." imageMso="AttachMenu" /> Configuration Schema Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Example Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. AttachTemplateOnOpen This is a command that is run on the Doucment Open Event. If the configuration setting AttachTemplateOnOpen is set to True then when a Document is opened the value in the Custom Document Property K+BTemplate is used as the Word template name to attach. The name of the custom doucment property can be changed by setting the configuration setting Default config JSON "AttachTemplateConfiguration": { "AttachTemplateOnOpen": false, "TemplatePropertyName": "K+BTemplate" }

licensing/overview.html.summary: Licensing If there is a valid Licence then the application will run in full mode. If there is no valid licence then the application will run in a limited mode. Activating a Licence Open the About Dialog from the Ribbon and click 'Activate'. Deactivating a Licence Open the About Dialog from the Ribbon and click 'Deactivate'.
